"Confectionery Festival eve event: flowers add sweetness to new government building -- Toyooka." {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers) |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = (trad.) {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}) |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 3rd Sunday of April |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Nakashima Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach]] '''Nakashima Shrine''' (なかしまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was prefectural shrine. Revered by confectionery makers as the "progenitor deity of sweets", it has branch shrines across Japan. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}, with {{ill|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami}} as associated deity. Tajimamori-no-Mikoto is said to be a fifth-generation descendant of Amenohiboko and, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} recorded in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}', brought back from {{ill|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|1=Tokoyo no kuni}} the "Tokijiku no Kagu no Mi" (the {{ill|lt=tachibana|Q11121938|WD=Q11121938|lt=tachibana|1=Tachibana orange}} citrus)Suinin chronicle, postscript section, 3rd month day 12 (corresponding to {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} year 2).. Because tachibana (prototype of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikan|1=UNKNOWN}} and other {{ill|lt=citrus fruits|Q3216053|WD=Q3216053|1=Citreae}}) was regarded as a top-class confection ingredient, he is revered as deity of confectionery and citrus ancestor deity{{Cite web|和書|title=Yoroku: "Tokoyo no Kuni" and ancient Japanese...|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20171227/ddm/001/070/112000c|website=Mainichi Shimbun|accessdate=2021-11-25|language=ja}}. He is also regarded as a sericulture deity because he is said to have settled at the present site, reclaimed the land, and encouraged {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}. Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami was deity of Ami Shrine, merged in the medieval period, and is ancestral deity of the Toritori no Muraji clan. According to the Nihon Shoki, he captured a swan for prince {{ill|lt=Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto|Q11631423|WD=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}} by imperial orderSuinin chronicle, year 23 entry.. One theory says he was enshrined near Wananami no Minato, where the swan was captured in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}However, the Kojiki does not name Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami; it says the swan was captured by Yamanobe no Ōtaka (Suinin record).. === History === According to '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'"Kokushi Monjo" is said to have been compiled in {{ill|lt=Ten'en|Q1327245|WD=Q1327245|1=Ten'en}} 3 (975) by a person called Yōkō Mashika, and is treated as doubtful, though some see old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, 'Amenohiboko,' Sōgeisha, 1966). See also "{{ill|lt=Koshi Koden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976|1=koshi koden}}"., in {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}), Miyake no Kishi, a seventh-generation descendant of Tajimamori, began enshrining Tajimamori as ancestral deity. The name "Nakashima" is explained by saying Tajimamori's tomb appears like an island in the pond of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Suinin's mausoleum|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the medieval period, four shrines in Ami-gō (Ariko Shrine, Amuka Shrine, Ami Shrine, and Kasumi Shrine) were merged and it was called "Gosha Daimyōjin," but later all except Ami Shrine (Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami) were separated. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) October it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}; in year 28 ({{ill|lt=1895|Q7844|WD=Q7844|1=1895}}) December it became {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|WD=Q18658|1=1935}}) October it was promoted to prefectural shrine. === Festival === The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on the third Sunday of April, also called the '''Confectionery Festival''' and '''Kikkasai''' (Tachibana Flower Festival). Many confectionery industry representatives from across Japan attend and pray for prosperity. === Shrine buildings === The honden is {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=niken-sha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roofing. After a fire during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} period ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}-{{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}), it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōchō|Q1193070|WD=Q1193070|1=Shōchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}). It is designated {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} as a structure well showing features of the mid-{{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. === Cultural properties === (Type and designation date in parentheses) ===Important Cultural Property (national)=== *Honden with 1 ridge tag (architecture; February 8, Meiji 45 ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077|1=1912}})) === Access === *From {{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}} on JR {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} to Okuno and get off at "Nakashima Jinja" stop, or take bus to {{ill|lt=Izushi|Q11395756|WD=Q11395756|1=Izushi}} via Kami and walk 15 minutes from "Morio-guchi" stop. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.19 San'indō 2, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * 'Hyōgoken Jinja-shi' lower vol., Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 Hyōgoken Shinshokukai edition) * 'Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture I' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * 'Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia,' Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7 === External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322083.html Nakashima Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Office) {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]][[Category:qq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was prefectural shrine. Revered by confectionery makers as the "progenitor deity of sweets", it has branch shrines across Japan. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}, with {{ill|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami}} as associated deity. Tajimamori-no-Mikoto is said to be a fifth-generation descendant of Amenohiboko and, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} recorded in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}', brought back from {{ill|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|1=Tokoyo no kuni}} the "Tokijiku no Kagu no Mi" (the {{ill|lt=tachibana|Q11121938|WD=Q11121938|lt=tachibana|1=Tachibana orange}} citrus)Suinin chronicle, postscript section, 3rd month day 12 (corresponding to {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} year 2).. Because tachibana (prototype of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikan|1=UNKNOWN}} and other {{ill|lt=citrus fruits|Q3216053|WD=Q3216053|1=Citreae}}) was regarded as a top-class confection ingredient, he is revered as deity of confectionery and citrus ancestor deity{{Cite web|和書|title=Yoroku: "Tokoyo no Kuni" and ancient Japanese...|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20171227/ddm/001/070/112000c|website=Mainichi Shimbun|accessdate=2021-11-25|language=ja}}. He is also regarded as a sericulture deity because he is said to have settled at the present site, reclaimed the land, and encouraged {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}. Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami was deity of Ami Shrine, merged in the medieval period, and is ancestral deity of the Toritori no Muraji clan. According to the Nihon Shoki, he captured a swan for prince {{ill|lt=Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto|Q11631423|WD=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}} by imperial orderSuinin chronicle, year 23 entry.. One theory says he was enshrined near Wananami no Minato, where the swan was captured in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}However, the Kojiki does not name Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami; it says the swan was captured by Yamanobe no Ōtaka (Suinin record).. === History === According to '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'"Kokushi Monjo" is said to have been compiled in {{ill|lt=Ten'en|Q1327245|WD=Q1327245|1=Ten'en}} 3 (975) by a person called Yōkō Mashika, and is treated as doubtful, though some see old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, 'Amenohiboko,' Sōgeisha, 1966). See also "{{ill|lt=Koshi Koden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976|1=koshi koden}}"., in {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}), Miyake no Kishi, a seventh-generation descendant of Tajimamori, began enshrining Tajimamori as ancestral deity. The name "Nakashima" is explained by saying Tajimamori's tomb appears like an island in the pond of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Suinin's mausoleum|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the medieval period, four shrines in Ami-gō (Ariko Shrine, Amuka Shrine, Ami Shrine, and Kasumi Shrine) were merged and it was called "Gosha Daimyōjin," but later all except Ami Shrine (Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami) were separated. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) October it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}; in year 28 ({{ill|lt=1895|Q7844|WD=Q7844|1=1895}}) December it became {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|WD=Q18658|1=1935}}) October it was promoted to prefectural shrine. === Festival === The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on the third Sunday of April, also called the '''Confectionery Festival''' and '''Kikkasai''' (Tachibana Flower Festival). Many confectionery industry representatives from across Japan attend and pray for prosperity. === Shrine buildings === The honden is {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=niken-sha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roofing. After a fire during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} period ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}-{{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}), it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōchō|Q1193070|WD=Q1193070|1=Shōchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}). It is designated {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} as a structure well showing features of the mid-{{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. === Cultural properties === (Type and designation date in parentheses) ===Important Cultural Property (national)=== *Honden with 1 ridge tag (architecture; February 8, Meiji 45 ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077|1=1912}})) === Access === *From {{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}} on JR {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} to Okuno and get off at "Nakashima Jinja" stop, or take bus to {{ill|lt=Izushi|Q11395756|WD=Q11395756|1=Izushi}} via Kami and walk 15 minutes from "Morio-guchi" stop. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.19 San'indō 2, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * 'Hyōgoken Jinja-shi' lower vol., Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 Hyōgoken Shinshokukai edition) * 'Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture I' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * 'Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia,' Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7 === External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322083.html Nakashima Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Office) {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]

== Japanese content == {{otheruses|shrine in Toyooka, Hyōgo Prefecture|other shrines with the same name|Nakashima Shrine}} {{神社 |名称 = Nakashima Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya haiden Hyogo001.jpg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}1 Miyake |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|31|32.9|N|134|51|45.0|E|scale:20000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nakashima Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}Toshihiro Hamamoto (April 20, 2014). "Confectionery Festival eve event: flowers add sweetness to new government building -- Toyooka." {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers) |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = (trad.) {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}) |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 3rd Sunday of April |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Nakashima Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach]] '''Nakashima Shrine''' (なかしまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was prefectural shrine. Revered by confectionery makers as the "progenitor deity of sweets", it has branch shrines across Japan. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}, with {{ill|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami}} as associated deity. Tajimamori-no-Mikoto is said to be a fifth-generation descendant of Amenohiboko and, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} recorded in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}', brought back from {{ill|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|1=Tokoyo no kuni}} the "Tokijiku no Kagu no Mi" (the {{ill|lt=tachibana|Q11121938|WD=Q11121938|lt=tachibana|1=Tachibana orange}} citrus)Suinin chronicle, postscript section, 3rd month day 12 (corresponding to {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} year 2).. Because tachibana (prototype of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikan|1=UNKNOWN}} and other {{ill|lt=citrus fruits|Q3216053|WD=Q3216053|1=Citreae}}) was regarded as a top-class confection ingredient, he is revered as deity of confectionery and citrus ancestor deity{{Cite web|和書|title=Yoroku: "Tokoyo no Kuni" and ancient Japanese...|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20171227/ddm/001/070/112000c|website=Mainichi Shimbun|accessdate=2021-11-25|language=ja}}. He is also regarded as a sericulture deity because he is said to have settled at the present site, reclaimed the land, and encouraged {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}. Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami was deity of Ami Shrine, merged in the medieval period, and is ancestral deity of the Toritori no Muraji clan. According to the Nihon Shoki, he captured a swan for prince {{ill|lt=Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto|Q11631423|WD=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}} by imperial orderSuinin chronicle, year 23 entry.. One theory says he was enshrined near Wananami no Minato, where the swan was captured in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}However, the Kojiki does not name Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami; it says the swan was captured by Yamanobe no Ōtaka (Suinin record).. === History === According to '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'"Kokushi Monjo" is said to have been compiled in {{ill|lt=Ten'en|Q1327245|WD=Q1327245|1=Ten'en}} 3 (975) by a person called Yōkō Mashika, and is treated as doubtful, though some see old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, 'Amenohiboko,' Sōgeisha, 1966). See also "{{ill|lt=Koshi Koden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976|1=koshi koden}}"., in {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}), Miyake no Kishi, a seventh-generation descendant of Tajimamori, began enshrining Tajimamori as ancestral deity. The name "Nakashima" is explained by saying Tajimamori's tomb appears like an island in the pond of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Suinin's mausoleum|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the medieval period, four shrines in Ami-gō (Ariko Shrine, Amuka Shrine, Ami Shrine, and Kasumi Shrine) were merged and it was called "Gosha Daimyōjin," but later all except Ami Shrine (Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami) were separated. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) October it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}; in year 28 ({{ill|lt=1895|Q7844|WD=Q7844|1=1895}}) December it became {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|WD=Q18658|1=1935}}) October it was promoted to prefectural shrine. === Festival === The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on the third Sunday of April, also called the '''Confectionery Festival''' and '''Kikkasai''' (Tachibana Flower Festival). Many confectionery industry representatives from across Japan attend and pray for prosperity. === Shrine buildings === The honden is {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=niken-sha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roofing. After a fire during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} period ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}-{{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}), it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōchō|Q1193070|WD=Q1193070|1=Shōchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}). It is designated {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} as a structure well showing features of the mid-{{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. === Cultural properties === (Type and designation date in parentheses) ===Important Cultural Property (national)=== *Honden with 1 ridge tag (architecture; February 8, Meiji 45 ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077|1=1912}})) === Access === *From {{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}} on JR {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} to Okuno and get off at "Nakashima Jinja" stop, or take bus to {{ill|lt=Izushi|Q11395756|WD=Q11395756|1=Izushi}} via Kami and walk 15 minutes from "Morio-guchi" stop. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.19 San'indō 2, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * 'Hyōgoken Jinja-shi' lower vol., Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 Hyōgoken Shinshokukai edition) * 'Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture I' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * 'Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia,' Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7 === External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322083.html Nakashima Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Office) {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]

former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = (trad.) {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}) |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 3rd Sunday of April |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Nakashima Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach]] '''Nakashima Shrine''' (なかしまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was prefectural shrine. Revered by confectionery makers as the "progenitor deity of sweets", it has branch shrines across Japan. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}, with {{ill|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami}} as associated deity. Tajimamori-no-Mikoto is said to be a fifth-generation descendant of Amenohiboko and, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} recorded in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}', brought back from {{ill|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|1=Tokoyo no kuni}} the "Tokijiku no Kagu no Mi" (the {{ill|lt=tachibana|Q11121938|WD=Q11121938|lt=tachibana|1=Tachibana orange}} citrus)Suinin chronicle, postscript section, 3rd month day 12 (corresponding to {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} year 2).. Because tachibana (prototype of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikan|1=UNKNOWN}} and other {{ill|lt=citrus fruits|Q3216053|WD=Q3216053|1=Citreae}}) was regarded as a top-class confection ingredient, he is revered as deity of confectionery and citrus ancestor deity{{Cite web|和書|title=Yoroku: "Tokoyo no Kuni" and ancient Japanese...|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20171227/ddm/001/070/112000c|website=Mainichi Shimbun|accessdate=2021-11-25|language=ja}}. He is also regarded as a sericulture deity because he is said to have settled at the present site, reclaimed the land, and encouraged {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}. Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami was deity of Ami Shrine, merged in the medieval period, and is ancestral deity of the Toritori no Muraji clan. According to the Nihon Shoki, he captured a swan for prince {{ill|lt=Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto|Q11631423|WD=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}} by imperial orderSuinin chronicle, year 23 entry.. One theory says he was enshrined near Wananami no Minato, where the swan was captured in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}However, the Kojiki does not name Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami; it says the swan was captured by Yamanobe no Ōtaka (Suinin record).. === History === According to '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'"Kokushi Monjo" is said to have been compiled in {{ill|lt=Ten'en|Q1327245|WD=Q1327245|1=Ten'en}} 3 (975) by a person called Yōkō Mashika, and is treated as doubtful, though some see old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, 'Amenohiboko,' Sōgeisha, 1966). See also "{{ill|lt=Koshi Koden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976|1=koshi koden}}"., in {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}), Miyake no Kishi, a seventh-generation descendant of Tajimamori, began enshrining Tajimamori as ancestral deity. The name "Nakashima" is explained by saying Tajimamori's tomb appears like an island in the pond of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Suinin's mausoleum|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the medieval period, four shrines in Ami-gō (Ariko Shrine, Amuka Shrine, Ami Shrine, and Kasumi Shrine) were merged and it was called "Gosha Daimyōjin," but later all except Ami Shrine (Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami) were separated. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) October it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}; in year 28 ({{ill|lt=1895|Q7844|WD=Q7844|1=1895}}) December it became {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|WD=Q18658|1=1935}}) October it was promoted to prefectural shrine. === Festival === The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on the third Sunday of April, also called the '''Confectionery Festival''' and '''Kikkasai''' (Tachibana Flower Festival). Many confectionery industry representatives from across Japan attend and pray for prosperity. === Shrine buildings === The honden is {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=niken-sha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roofing. After a fire during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} period ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}-{{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}), it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōchō|Q1193070|WD=Q1193070|1=Shōchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}). It is designated {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} as a structure well showing features of the mid-{{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. === Cultural properties === (Type and designation date in parentheses) ===Important Cultural Property (national)=== *Honden with 1 ridge tag (architecture; February 8, Meiji 45 ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077|1=1912}})) === Access === *From {{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}} on JR {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} to Okuno and get off at "Nakashima Jinja" stop, or take bus to {{ill|lt=Izushi|Q11395756|WD=Q11395756|1=Izushi}} via Kami and walk 15 minutes from "Morio-guchi" stop. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.19 San'indō 2, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * 'Hyōgoken Jinja-shi' lower vol., Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 Hyōgoken Shinshokukai edition) * 'Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture I' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * 'Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia,' Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7 === External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322083.html Nakashima Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Office) {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]

== Japanese content == {{otheruses|shrine in Toyooka, Hyōgo Prefecture|other shrines with the same name|Nakashima Shrine}} {{神社 |名称 = Nakashima Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya haiden Hyogo001.jpg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}1 Miyake |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|31|32.9|N|134|51|45.0|E|scale:20000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nakashima Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}Toshihiro Hamamoto (April 20, 2014). former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = (trad.) {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}) |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 3rd Sunday of April |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Nakashima Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach]] '''Nakashima Shrine''' (なかしまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was prefectural shrine. Revered by confectionery makers as the "progenitor deity of sweets", it has branch shrines across Japan. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}, with {{ill|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami}} as associated deity. Tajimamori-no-Mikoto is said to be a fifth-generation descendant of Amenohiboko and, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} recorded in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}', brought back from {{ill|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|1=Tokoyo no kuni}} the "Tokijiku no Kagu no Mi" (the {{ill|lt=tachibana|Q11121938|WD=Q11121938|lt=tachibana|1=Tachibana orange}} citrus)Suinin chronicle, postscript section, 3rd month day 12 (corresponding to {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} year 2).. Because tachibana (prototype of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikan|1=UNKNOWN}} and other {{ill|lt=citrus fruits|Q3216053|WD=Q3216053|1=Citreae}}) was regarded as a top-class confection ingredient, he is revered as deity of confectionery and citrus ancestor deity{{Cite web|和書|title=Yoroku: "Tokoyo no Kuni" and ancient Japanese...|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20171227/ddm/001/070/112000c|website=Mainichi Shimbun|accessdate=2021-11-25|language=ja}}. He is also regarded as a sericulture deity because he is said to have settled at the present site, reclaimed the land, and encouraged {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}. Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami was deity of Ami Shrine, merged in the medieval period, and is ancestral deity of the Toritori no Muraji clan. According to the Nihon Shoki, he captured a swan for prince {{ill|lt=Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto|Q11631423|WD=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}} by imperial orderSuinin chronicle, year 23 entry.. One theory says he was enshrined near Wananami no Minato, where the swan was captured in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}However, the Kojiki does not name Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami; it says the swan was captured by Yamanobe no Ōtaka (Suinin record).. === History === According to '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'"Kokushi Monjo" is said to have been compiled in {{ill|lt=Ten'en|Q1327245|WD=Q1327245|1=Ten'en}} 3 (975) by a person called Yōkō Mashika, and is treated as doubtful, though some see old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, 'Amenohiboko,' Sōgeisha, 1966). See also "{{ill|lt=Koshi Koden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976|1=koshi koden}}"., in {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|WD=Q31043|1=606}}), Miyake no Kishi, a seventh-generation descendant of Tajimamori, began enshrining Tajimamori as ancestral deity. The name "Nakashima" is explained by saying Tajimamori's tomb appears like an island in the pond of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Suinin's mausoleum|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the medieval period, four shrines in Ami-gō (Ariko Shrine, Amuka Shrine, Ami Shrine, and Kasumi Shrine) were merged and it was called "Gosha Daimyōjin," but later all except Ami Shrine (Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami) were separated. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) October it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}; in year 28 ({{ill|lt=1895|Q7844|WD=Q7844|1=1895}}) December it became {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|WD=Q18658|1=1935}}) October it was promoted to prefectural shrine. === Festival === The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on the third Sunday of April, also called the '''Confectionery Festival''' and '''Kikkasai''' (Tachibana Flower Festival). Many confectionery industry representatives from across Japan attend and pray for prosperity. === Shrine buildings === The honden is {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=niken-sha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roofing. After a fire during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} period ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398|1=1394}}-{{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}), it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōchō|Q1193070|WD=Q1193070|1=Shōchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315|1=1428}}). It is designated {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} as a structure well showing features of the mid-{{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. === Cultural properties === (Type and designation date in parentheses) ===Important Cultural Property (national)=== *Honden with 1 ridge tag (architecture; February 8, Meiji 45 ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077|1=1912}})) === Access === *From {{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}} on JR {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} to Okuno and get off at "Nakashima Jinja" stop, or take bus to {{ill|lt=Izushi|Q11395756|WD=Q11395756|1=Izushi}} via Kami and walk 15 minutes from "Morio-guchi" stop. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.19 San'indō 2, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * 'Hyōgoken Jinja-shi' lower vol., Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 Hyōgoken Shinshokukai edition) * 'Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture I' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * 'Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia,' Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7 === External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322083.html Nakashima Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Office) {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]

== Japanese content == {{otheruses|shrine in Toyooka, Hyōgo Prefecture|other shrines with the same name|Nakashima Shrine}} {{神社 |名称 = Nakashima Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya haiden Hyogo001.jpg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|qid=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}1 Miyake |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|31|32.9|N|134|51|45.0|E|scale:20000_region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nakashima Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|qid=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}Toshihiro Hamamoto (April 20, 2014). "Confectionery Festival eve event: flowers add sweetness to new government building -- Toyooka." {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|qid=Q1136866|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers) |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} |創建 = (trad.) {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|qid=Q31043|1=606}}) |本殿 = Niken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 3rd Sunday of April |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Nakashima Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Nakashima-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach]] '''Nakashima Shrine''' (なかしまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|qid=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, and its former rank was prefectural shrine. Revered by confectionery makers as the "progenitor deity of sweets", it has branch shrines across Japan. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Tajimamori|Q11316133|qid=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori-no-Mikoto|1=Tajimamori}}, with {{ill|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami}} as associated deity. Tajimamori-no-Mikoto is said to be a fifth-generation descendant of Amenohiboko and, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} recorded in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}', brought back from {{ill|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|Q11060259|qid=Q11060259|1=Tokoyo no kuni}} the "Tokijiku no Kagu no Mi" (the {{ill|lt=tachibana|Q11121938|qid=Q11121938|lt=tachibana|1=Tachibana orange}} citrus)Suinin chronicle, postscript section, 3rd month day 12 (corresponding to {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} year 2).. Because tachibana (prototype of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mikan|1=UNKNOWN}} and other {{ill|lt=citrus fruits|Q3216053|qid=Q3216053|1=Citreae}}) was regarded as a top-class confection ingredient, he is revered as deity of confectionery and citrus ancestor deity{{Cite web|和書|title=Yoroku: "Tokoyo no Kuni" and ancient Japanese...|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20171227/ddm/001/070/112000c|website=Mainichi Shimbun|accessdate=2021-11-25|language=ja}}. He is also regarded as a sericulture deity because he is said to have settled at the present site, reclaimed the land, and encouraged {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}. Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami was deity of Ami Shrine, merged in the medieval period, and is ancestral deity of the Toritori no Muraji clan. According to the Nihon Shoki, he captured a swan for prince {{ill|lt=Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto|Q11631423|qid=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}} by imperial orderSuinin chronicle, year 23 entry.. One theory says he was enshrined near Wananami no Minato, where the swan was captured in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}However, the Kojiki does not name Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami; it says the swan was captured by Yamanobe no Ōtaka (Suinin record).. === History === According to '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'"Kokushi Monjo" is said to have been compiled in {{ill|lt=Ten'en|Q1327245|qid=Q1327245|1=Ten'en}} 3 (975) by a person called Yōkō Mashika, and is treated as doubtful, though some see old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, 'Amenohiboko,' Sōgeisha, 1966). See also "{{ill|lt=Koshi Koden|Q11410976|qid=Q11410976|1=koshi koden}}"., in {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} 15 ({{ill|lt=606|Q31043|qid=Q31043|1=606}}), Miyake no Kishi, a seventh-generation descendant of Tajimamori, began enshrining Tajimamori as ancestral deity. The name "Nakashima" is explained by saying Tajimamori's tomb appears like an island in the pond of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Suinin's mausoleum|1=UNKNOWN}}. In the medieval period, four shrines in Ami-gō (Ariko Shrine, Amuka Shrine, Ami Shrine, and Kasumi Shrine) were merged and it was called "Gosha Daimyōjin," but later all except Ami Shrine (Amenoyukawatano-no-Kami) were separated. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) October it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}; in year 28 ({{ill|lt=1895|Q7844|qid=Q7844|1=1895}}) December it became {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|qid=Q18658|1=1935}}) October it was promoted to prefectural shrine. === Festival === The {{ill|lt=annual festival|Q11385469|qid=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on the third Sunday of April, also called the '''Confectionery Festival''' and '''Kikkasai''' (Tachibana Flower Festival). Many confectionery industry representatives from across Japan attend and pray for prosperity. === Shrine buildings === The honden is {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|lt=niken-sha nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with {{ill|lt=hiwadabuki|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roofing. After a fire during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} period ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|qid=Q6398|1=1394}}-{{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|qid=Q6315|1=1428}}), it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōchō|Q1193070|qid=Q1193070|1=Shōchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|qid=Q6315|1=1428}}). It is designated {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} as a structure well showing features of the mid-{{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. === Cultural properties === (Type and designation date in parentheses) ===Important Cultural Property (national)=== *Honden with 1 ridge tag (architecture; February 8, Meiji 45 ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|qid=Q2077|1=1912}})) === Access === *From {{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)|Q871612|qid=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}} on JR {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|qid=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|qid=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} to Okuno and get off at "Nakashima Jinja" stop, or take bus to {{ill|lt=Izushi|Q11395756|qid=Q11395756|1=Izushi}} via Kami and walk 15 minutes from "Morio-guchi" stop. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.19 San'indō 2, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * 'Hyōgoken Jinja-shi' lower vol., Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 Hyōgoken Shinshokukai edition) * 'Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture I' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * 'Hyōgo Prefecture Encyclopedia,' Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7 === External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322083.html Nakashima Shrine] (Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Office) {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakashima Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]
